column3: Rationale

The derivation of a mathematical model starts with a small change in position, velocity or mass which has taken place in a short time, contrained by  a physical principle (e.g a conservation law), which must be satisfied. The limiting form of the principle is then formulated as a relationship between derivatives. The ability to calculate a derivative from its definition as a limit is crucial.
Basic limits of trigfunctions (e.g. sin theta approx theta) are ubiquitous, as is  the use of vectors, tangents, and normals.
Graphical reasoning (e.g. free body diagrams) dominates the translation of physical models into equations.

This is the calculus of moving particles parametrized by time in two and three dimensions. By treating special cases and doing the calculus componentwise, one can avoid the vector calculus of line integrals and gradients.
Motion under central forces requires computing velocity and accelaration in polar, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates.

Implicit differentiation is used to calculate the relationship between derivatives in cartesian coordinates and those in polar coordinates. Students routinely use these formulas, but are not asked to derive them.
Calcluating velocity and acceleration from a formula for the path of a particle requires basic differentiation formulas.
Calculating velocity and displacement based on forces that determine particle acceleration requires computing definite integrals.
Analysis of periodic motion requires the use of  trigonometric functions and their derivatives.

The paths of a objects accelerated  by central forces are conic sections. Descriptions of planetary motion require the use of  polar coordinate and motivate descriptions of ellipses using eccentricity.
Newton's second law translates forces into acceleration. That acceleration must be integrated to  find velocity and integrated again to find displacement.
The fundamental theorem of calculus provides the link between derivatives an integrals, thereby the link between acceleration and  velocity, and then displacements.

column1: Indefinite integrals and the net change theorem
column3: This is another formulation of the fundamental theorem of calculus, which also serves as a link between acceleration and  velocity, and then displacements.

Describing the motion of objects accelerated by central forces requires expressing acceleration,velocity, and displacement in polar, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates, as well as well as calcualating the derivatives and integras that relate them..
Work is the integral of force with respect to  displacement (distance).

column1: Indefinite integrals and the net change theorem
column3: Work is the net change in Energy caused by the action (integral) of force over distance.

Force is the derivative of (minus) the potential energy with respect to displacement, so the change in the  potential energy is the integral of the force, which is an expression of the fundamental theorem of calculus.

column1: Indefinite integrals and the net change theorem
column2: Potentials, conservativation of energy, and momentum
column3: Impulse is the  change in momentum with respect to time, which equals the integral of force with respect to time, yet another expression of the net change theorem.

column2: Potentials, conservativation of energy, and momentum
column3: Calculating work requires integrating force with respect to displacement. The change of variables dx = vdt relates the work integral to the impulse integral.

column2: Dynamics of Rigid Bodies and Angular Momentum
column3: Evaluating cross products to calculate angular momentum (componentwise to keep calculations simple) requires the calculus of trigonometric functions

Calculating the center of mass and moment of inertia of an object requires evaluating multidimensional integrals. In important special cases (e.g disks rings bars) these integrals become ond dimensional area type integrals.

column1: Indefinite integrals and the net change theorem
column3: The multidimensional integrals required to compute the center of mass and moment of inertia for disks and rings often reduce to one dimensional integrals in polar coordinates"

column2: Dynamics of Rigid Bodies and Angular Momentum
column3: Calculating the linear and angular velocities and accelerations of rolling wheels and pulleys requires the calculus of trigonometric functions.

column2: Dynamics of Rigid Bodies and Angular Momentum
column3: Relating linear and angular velocity and acceleration requires calculus in polar coordinates.

column2: Dynamics of Rigid Bodies and Angular Momentum
column3: Pulleys pull cables attached to masses. Rolling wheels have related angular and linear velocity (of the center or the point of contact). The relation between these rates of change is a related rates problem.

Lagrangian dynamics that is based on the calculus of variations. It calculates the  particle dynamics (e.g the path of an object) as a stationary point of the Lagrangian  Action integral. Although it takes place in a much more sophisticated regime, the methods for finding that stationary point are esseentially the same as those in one dimensional optimaization problems.
The  Lagrangian Action is the definte integral of the Lagrangian function.

Finding the equations of motion requires calclulating the derivative of the action integral. Although this derivative is part of the more advanced  calculus of variations, it follows exactly the same line of reasoning as in the defintion of the derivative of a funtion of a single variable.
Integration by parts in the derivative of the action integral that characterizes the stationary points  results in a  differential equation known as the Equations of Motion.
The Equations of Motion differential equation is, in many cases, an ordinary differential equation.

column1: Limits at infinity and infinite limits
column3: Separating forced vibrations into transient and steady state solutions requires calulating the limiting behavior of a function as time approaches infinity.
Deriving formulas for resonant vibrations requires calculating limits of functions as the frequency approaches a resonant frequency.
L'Hopital's rule provides the most efficient (if not always the most transparent)  way to evaluate the limits that describe resonant vibrations.
Vibrations of a single mass or particle are periodic motions modelled by linear ordinary differential equations.
Vibrations are typically described by  trigonometric functions (as functions of time rather than angle).
A substantial amount of physical modelling and approximation is required to formulate the differential equations  that describe vibrations.
